coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots-coated CuFe2O4 nanocomposites as a reusable catalyst for highly efficient reduction of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jie
Yu, Binglong
Wang, Wenlong
Cai, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a carbon dots (CDs)-coated CuFe2O4 (CuFe2O4-CDs) nanocomposite consisting of magnetic CuFe2O4 core and CDs shell was synthesized via a facile one-pot solvothermal strategy. The CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite was characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, TGA and VSM. Due to the protection of the CDs layer, the CuFe2O4-CDs exhibits excellent reusability and outstanding catalytic activity which is far superior to similar materials in catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), Methylene blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB). The results suggested that the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites have promising potential as an efficient and reusable catalyst for water environment treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-coated CuFe2O4
Nanocomposite
Catalyst
Organic pollutants

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and HRTEM image (B) of CuFe2O4-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of 4-NP after addition of NaBH4 solution (A) and after addition of NaBH4 and CuFe2O4-CDs (B). (C) pseudo-first-order plots of ln(C/C0) versus reaction time for reducing 4-NP with different catalysts. (D) Time of 50% conversion versus reaction cycle for reducing 4-NP using CuFe2O4 and CuFe2O4-CDs catalysts.
The catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP with NaBH4 and CuFe2O4-CDs catalyst.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots-coated CuFe2O4 nanocomposites as a reusable catalyst for highly efficient reduction of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Engineering & Technology Research Center of Topic Precise Drug Delivery System, School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Engineering & Technology Research Center of Topic Precise Drug Delivery System

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a carbon dots (CDs)-coated CuFe2O4 (CuFe2O4-CDs) nanocomposite consisting of magnetic CuFe2O4 core and CDs shell was synthesized via a facile one-pot solvothermal strategy. The CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite was characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, TGA and VSM. Due to the protection of the CDs layer, the CuFe2O4-CDs exhibits excellent reusability and outstanding catalytic activity which is far superior to similar materials in catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), Methylene blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB). The results suggested that the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites have promising potential as an efficient and reusable catalyst for water environment treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, the problem of industrial wastewater pollution is increasingly severe and affecting human health and survival. Among the pollutants, nitrophenols are recognized as one of the most prevalent and serious organic pollutants produced in industrial and agricultural wastewater. Due to its low biodegradability in the environment and low solubility in water, 4-nitrophenol has been listed by the US Environmental Protection Agency as one of the most hazardous organic pollutants [1]. So far, various techniques have been reported to remove 4-nitrophenol from aqueous solutions, such as adsorption, catalytic reduction, photodegradation and membrane filtration [2–5]. Among these techniques, catalytic reduction is considered to be rapid, simple, efficient and low-cost, and its reduction product is 4-aminophenol (4-AP), which is less lethal to surrounding species and potentially more biodegradable in the environment [6–8]. 4-AP is also is widely used as an important intermediate in pharmaceutical industry [9]. Hence, the catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP is not only a critical step in industrial production, but also an important method to treat wastewater containing 4-NP.

Recently, magnetic nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention due to their outstanding and low-cost separation properties [10,11]. Among them, magnetic MFe2O4 (M denotes a transition metal, such as Cu and Zn) nanomaterial shows great potential in environmental treatment, owing to its excellent catalytic activity, adsorption capacity and superparamagnetism [12,13]. For example, Feng et al. first used CuFe2O4 as a magnetic catalyst for reducing 4-NP and achieved satisfactory results [14]. Deficiently, the structure of CuFe2O4 is easily destroyed by NaBH4 in the catalytic process, which leads to a serious decline of catalytic capacity in recycling.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterial, is consist of carbon core and a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [15]. Due to its outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, and high solubility, CDs-based nanocomposites have been extensively studied and have achieved good results in bioimaging, chemical sensors and photocatalysts [16–18]. In order to solve the problem that MFe2O4 is broken by NaBH4 during the catalytic process, Guo et al. used an ultrasonic strategy to combine the prepared CDs and MFe2O4 to obtain CDs-coated MFe2O4 nanocomposites [19]. The CDs-coated MFe2O4 nanocomposites have a structure in which CDs are attached to the MFe2O4 core, and thus form CDs/MFe2O4 nanocomposites. Although the nanocomposites exhibit enhanced catalytic activity and stability, it is limited in mass production due to its multi-step and complicated preparation process. Therefore, exploring a new method for preparing CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite with high catalytic activity and stability in one-step is urgently needed.

Herein, we developed a one-pot solvothermal strategy to fabricate a magnetic multifunctional CDs-coated CuFe2O4 nanocomposite catalyst with excellent catalytic activity, stability and applicability. The obtained CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite was characterized in terms of morphology, structure, and magnetic properties by TEM, XRD, XPS, TGA and VSM. Furthermore, the catalytic performance of nanocomposite in reducing 4-NP and degrading organic dyes was evaluated.

0.17 g CuCl2·2H2O, 0.54 g FeCl3·6H2O and 3.6 g NaAc were dissolved in 40 mL of ethylene glycol under bath sonication to form a clear solution. Subsequently, 0.6 g citric acid monohydrate (CA) as carbon source was introduced to the above solution. The resulting green solution was sonicated for 30 min and then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 190° for 10 h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resultant black solid product was collected by a powerful magnet and then washed alternately with deionized water and absolute ethanol several times. Finally, the solid product was dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

Firstly, 2.5 mL of 4-NP aqueous solution (0.15 mmol/L) was mixed with 0.5 mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.5 mol/L) in a standard quartz cuvette. Thereafter, 50 uL of 1 mg/mL catalyst suspension was poured into the mixed solution to start the reaction. The catalytic process was immediately recorded using UV–Vis absorption spectra (UV-2550, Shimadzu) in the scanning range of 250–500 nm. The catalytic performance of the samples were measured by monitoring the absorbance change at λmax = 400 nm. To research the reusability of the catalyst, the used sample was collected from the reaction system by using a magnet and washed with ultrapure water for the next cycle. Additionally, the catalytic activities of CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite for reducing MB/RhB under the same reaction conditions were also investigated by monitoring the absorbance change at λmax = 664 nm for MB, and λmax = 554 nm for RhB.

The sample was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 64250xi), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, PerkinElmer TGA 4000) and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, PPMS-9).

The morphology and crystalline structure of CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites were depicted by TEM and HRTEM images. From Fig. S1, the diameter of pure CuFe2O4 nanocrystalline particle is about 5.5 nm. When citric acid was introduced into the reaction system, the diameter of CuFe2O4 nanocrystalline particle decreases to 3.5 nm (Fig. 1A). Moreover, most of the CuFe2O4 nanocrystalline particles are in contact with each other and are coated with low contrast continuous CDs layers to show a core-shell structure (Fig. S2). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CuFe2O4-CDs in Fig. 1B clearly shows lattice fringe spacing of 0.25 nm, which match well with the (211) plane of CuFe2O4. On the surface of CuFe2O4, a clear outer shell of CDs layer was observed, indicating that CDs were successfully attached to the surface of CuFe2O4 nanoparticles to form a core-shell structure.

To further confirm whether CDs are present in the outer shell of nanocomposite, XRD and XPS were performed. The XRD pattern of CuFe2O4-CDs is shown in Fig. S3, five peaks at 2θ = 29.8°, 36.1°, 42.9°, 62.1° and 73.8° are indexed to the (112), (211), (220), (224) and (413) crystal planes of CuFe2O4 (JCPDS No. 34–0425), respectively. In addition, an impurity peak (marked as *) related to Cu (JCPDS No. 85–1326) are clearly observed. The presence of metallic copper is due to the use of ethylene glycol with strong reducing capability as a solvent, which causes some of Cu2+ to be reduced to metallic copper in the formation process of CuFe2O4 [20]. More importantly, a weak peak at 2θ value of 24.6° can be observed, which corresponding to CA derived CDs [21]. This result demonstrates the existence of CDs on the surface of CuFe2O4, suggesting the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized. Furthermore, the XPS analysis was performed to identify the composition details of the CuFe2O4-CDs. Fig. S4A shows the survey XPS spectra, four main peaks with binding energy of 285, 532, 712 and 935 eV are in accordance with C 1 s, O 1 s, Fe 2p and Cu 2p, respectively, indicating the coexistence of C, O, Fe and Cu elements in the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites. For the high resolution XPS of Cu 2p regions (Fig. S4B), the binding energy of Cu 2p3/2 is located at 933.7 eV with a shake-up satellite at 943.5 eV, and Cu 2p1/2 is observed at 953.5 eV with a satellite at 962.9 eV, and these two shake-up peaks can prove the presence of Cu2+ [22]. In addition, the Cu 2p3/2 peak be divided into two peaks at 933.1 and 934.2 eV, which may be attributed to Cu0 and Cu2+, respectively [23,24]. This indicates that metallic copper exists on the surface of the nanocomposite, which is consistent with the results of XRD. The Fe 2p spectra (Fig. S4C) show two main peaks at binding energies of 711.1 and 724.5 eV, which are assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 for Fe3+ [25]. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s is shown in Fig. S4D. The peaks located at 284.7, 286.0 and 288.1 eV are characteristics of CC, C-OH and CO groups of CDs, respectively [26]. Therefore, these results fully confirm the formation of CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites.

The contents of organic components in CuFe2O4 and CuFe2O4-CDs were quantified by TGA. The results are shown in Fig. S5A, the weight loss of the two samples before 150 °C can be attributed to the dehydration of moisture within the materials. As for CuFe2O4, the weight loss of 13% in the temperature range of 150–500 °C is due to the burning-off of adsorbed ethylene glycol in the sample. Furthermore, for CuFe2O4-CDs, the weight loss at 150–500 °C is about 18%, which has more weight loss than CuFe2O4. The weight loss of CuFe2O4-CDs is not only attributed to the burning-off of ethylene glycol but also the degradation of CDs. The content of CDs in the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposite is calculated to be about 5%.

The saturation magnetization strength of CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites were measured to be 17.3 emu g−1 (Fig. S5B), which is lower than the reported 45 emu g−1 for CuFe2O4 nanoparticles [14]. The declining magnetic properties are due to the contribution of the non-magnetic of the CDs. However, the inset pattern indicates that the nanocomposites can be easily attracted toward the magnet side in aqueous solution. Therefore, the CuFe2O4-CDs nanocomposites can be recycled for reuse.

Furthermore, to investigate the universal applicability of CuFe2O4-CDs for catalyzing other dyes, MB and RhB were selected as probe molecule to further evaluate the catalytic activity of the nanocomposites. Fig. S7A shows the plot of C/C0 of MB and RhB versus reaction time catalyzed by the CuFe2O4-CDs in the presence of NaBH4, respectively. In Fig. S7C and D, the degradation rate of both dyes exceeds 98% and RhB completes degradation in 20 s and MB only requires 8 s. The rate constant k of MB and RhB were calculated to be 0.565 and 0.191 s−1 (Fig. S7B), respectively. These results indicate that CuFe2O4-CDs has universal applicability and high catalytic activity, which is far superior to similar products (Table S3), thus showing more advantages in practical applications.

In summary, we presented a simple and efficient one-pot solvothermal method for the synthesis of magnetic core-shell CDs-coated CuFe2O4 nanocomposite. The CDs shell layer plays an important role in protecting the CuFe2O4 nanoparticles for reuse and enhancing catalytic activity of the samples for reducing 4-NP. In addition, the CuFe2O4-CDs possess high universal applicability, which also exhibits excellent catalytic performance in reducing MB/RhB dyes. Thanks to the simple preparation process, good catalytic activity, excellent stability and high applicability, CuFe2O4-CDs is suitable for mass production and has great potential for environmental purification.
We thank the support by the Project of Innovation for Enhancing Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Provincial Experimental Teaching Demonstration Center of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.catcom.2019.04.012.
